narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 37, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 68.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that stood out the most to the model. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers who passed the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% versus the average around the 6th decile, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential factor, suggesting early struggles that carried weight in the model's eyes. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 37—the second-most important factor—nudged the prediction toward success, as it's slightly above the average of 36.7 and aligns with stronger bar passers. While your full-time status matches what 93% of students choose and your family income is solidly upper-middle, these didn't sway the balance enough against your decile rankings. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having a competitive LSAT but lagging in GPA-related school rankings, which the model sees as the biggest hurdles to bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive—many in similar situations have improved dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to boost your academic deciles, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapply after building a stronger upward trajectory. You might also seek feedback from pre-law advisors familiar with diverse applicants like yourself to address these specific gaps. With focused effort, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
